pubmed_links: [Bhalla et al. (2008) Alterations in CDH15 and KIRREL3 in patients with mild to severe intellectual disability](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19012874/); [Martin et al. (2015) The intellectual disability gene Kirrel3 regulates target-specific mossy fiber synapse development in the hippocampus](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26575286/); [Taylor et al. (2020) Kirrel3-Mediated Synapse Formation Is Attenuated by Disease-Associated Missense Variants](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32503885/)
summary: KIRREL3, a synaptic cell-adhesion molecule of the immunoglobulin superfamily, is a well-documented risk gene for intellectual disability and autism spectrum disorder, with rare missense variants identified in patient cohorts; functional studies show it regulates target-specific mossy fiber synapse development in the hippocampus, and disease-associated missense variants attenuate its synaptogenic activity. (well-established)
